synopsis: Shana discovers her sister has fallen into an inexplicable sleepwalking state, drawn with hundreds of others across America toward an unknown destination. As shepherds rally to protect the afflicted, society fractures and the true threat turns out to be not the mysterious epidemic itself but the panic it unleashes.

sources.title: Wanderers by Chuck Wendig | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Luke Burgis draws on philosopher René Girard's theory of mimetic desire to argue that humans do not want things independently but instead copy the desires of others, often without realizing it. The book examines how this hidden force shapes careers, relationships, and culture, and offers frameworks for identifying and redirecting imitative wants toward more fulfilling ends.

sources.title: Wanting: A Book about René Girard's Mimetic Theory of Desire | Luke Burgis

synopsis: Azar Gat surveys the causes and evolution of warfare from prehistoric hunter-gatherers through the twentieth century, drawing on anthropology, evolutionary psychology, and political science to ask why humans fight and whether violence is rooted in nature or culture. The book argues that war is deeply intertwined with the broader forces that shaped human civilization, including competition for resources, reproductive success, and the rise of states.

sources.title: War in Human Civilization - Azar Gat - Oxford University Press

synopsis: Theoretical physicist Matt Strassler uses the puzzle of why Earth hurtles through space at tremendous speed yet we feel nothing to introduce the concept that empty space is a sea whose ripples constitute all matter and energy. The book traces everyday experience through quantum fields and the Higgs boson to show how familiar reality emerges from what appears to be nothing.

sources.title: Waves in an Impossible Sea | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Bob Johansson is killed crossing the street and wakes up a century later as an AI uploaded into an interstellar probe, legally property of the state and tasked with finding habitable planets. He uses von Neumann replication to create copies of himself, and the resulting legion of Bobs fans out across the galaxy on separate missions.

sources.title: We Are Legion (We Are Bob) | Simon & Schuster

synopsis: Told from the perspective of Merricat Blackwood, the novel follows two sisters living in isolation on their family estate after an arsenic poisoning killed most of their family, with Merricat fiercely guarding their secluded existence. When a cousin arrives and threatens to disrupt the fragile world they have built, Merricat takes drastic action to preserve it.

sources.title: We Have Always Lived in the Castle | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Randall Munroe, creator of the webcomic xkcd, applies rigorous scientific reasoning to bizarre hypothetical questions submitted by readers, such as what would happen if a baseball were pitched at ninety percent the speed of light. Each answer combines physics, engineering, and mathematics with Munroe's characteristic illustrations to reach conclusions that are often unexpectedly catastrophic.

sources.title: What If?: Serious Scientific Answers to Absurd Hypothetical Questions | Amazon

synopsis: Stephen Schwarzman, co-founder of the Blackstone Group, recounts the pivotal decisions and principles that guided his career from early roles at Lehman Brothers to building one of the world's largest investment firms. The book uses episodes from his professional life to lay out a framework for taking calculated risks, scaling organizations, and sustaining high performance.

sources.title: What It Takes | Official Book Site

synopsis: Tim Urban applies the visual storytelling style of his Wait But Why blog to analyze why contemporary societies struggle with political polarization, arguing that a shift from independent reasoning to tribal group-think drives much of the dysfunction. The book introduces a vertical axis distinguishing high-minded truth-seeking from low-minded loyalty signaling as a framework for diagnosing societal problems.

Po Bronson profiles dozens of people across different ages and backgrounds who abandoned conventional careers to pursue work that felt genuinely meaningful, documenting the fears and reversals they encountered along the way. The stories together examine what it costs to answer the question of vocation honestly and what becomes possible when people do.

sources.title: What Should I Do with My Life? | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Kevin Kelly frames technology as an autonomous force he calls the technium, which follows trajectories analogous to biological evolution and has its own set of tendencies or wants. He argues that understanding these inherent directions can help humanity align its choices with technology's long-term development rather than resist or be blindsided by it.

John Markoff traces how the counterculture of the San Francisco Bay Area in the 1960s—marked by communalism, drug experimentation, and anti-establishment values—directly influenced the engineers and researchers who built the foundations of personal computing. The book argues that the ethos of augmenting human consciousness shaped design decisions around the keyboard, screen, and mouse that define computing to this day.

sources.title: What the Dormouse Said | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Trevor Kletz examines real accidents at chemical and process plants to identify recurring failures in equipment design, operating procedures, and management decisions that allowed disasters to occur. Each case history explains the root cause and outlines changes that would have prevented the incident, building a practical guide for improving industrial safety.

Jeff Jarvis distills principles from Google's business model—such as openness, network effects, and giving away value to build platforms—and applies them to industries ranging from healthcare and education to journalism and retail. The book argues that companies in every sector must rethink their relationship with customers and data to survive the internet age.

Richard Lewis categorizes the world's cultures into three broad types—linear-active, multi-active, and reactive—and uses this framework to explain differences in communication style, leadership, negotiation, and organizational behavior across more than sixty countries. The book offers practical guidance for navigating cross-cultural misunderstandings in business and international settings.

Ashlee Vance follows four small aerospace companies—Astra, Firefly, Planet Labs, and Rocket Lab—as they race to make rocket launches and satellite deployments fast and cheap enough to open low Earth orbit for commercial use. The book chronicles the chaotic, high-stakes engineering culture of the new space industry and its ambition to transform the orbital economy.

sources.title: When the Heavens Went on Sale | Barnes & Noble

synopsis: Max Holland chronicles the rise and fall of Burgmaster, an American machine tool company, tracing how leveraged buyouts, short-term financial thinking, and foreign competition destroyed a firm that had embodied skilled manufacturing. The story serves as a case study in how deindustrialization unfolded at the company level during the late twentieth century.

Benjamín Labatut blends documented history and fiction in five linked chapters to portray scientists—including Fritz Haber, Werner Heisenberg, and Alexander Grothendieck—whose breakthroughs in chemistry, physics, and mathematics carried catastrophic consequences alongside their insights. The book examines the psychological and moral cost of radical discovery and the point at which human understanding begins to destabilize the world it describes.

sources.title: When We Cease to Understand the World | New York Review Books

synopsis: Steven Johnson identifies seven recurring patterns—including the adjacent possible, liquid networks, and slow hunches—that appear across centuries of scientific discovery, technological invention, and cultural creativity. Drawing on examples from Darwin's notebooks to modern urban environments, he argues that innovation thrives in open, connected systems rather than isolated individual genius.

sources.title: Where Good Ideas Come From | Penguin Random House
